posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
Our Monday Night Blind Wine Dinner Group enjoyed another fun and fabulous evening at our stellar venue, Ca Dario Montecito.

Each of the 4 of us were pre-assigned wines to bring and as usual, we had 1 bubbly, 1 white and 2 reds. Most of our table conversation was about each wine and we explored possibilities and choices with reasons for our guesstimates and also as usual, we had another series of learning experiences.

Here’s some notes:

2012 LOUIS ROEDERER BRUT- blind; this was bottle-aged for 4 years before being left to rest for 6 more months after disgorging; dosage was 9 gpl; 70% Pinot Noir, 30% Chardonnay; the color was light straw yellow; my first impressions included it had high acidity, perhaps citrus fruit contributed and it’s feel good frothy mousse and additional flavors that included mango, honeysuckle and buttered popcorn; once tasted, I also got some green apple early and yellow apple late, both with a coat of honey and then much later, some lemon and lime that defined the citrus element; it was rich, delicious and stately; even though I did not get any red fruit, it seemed to be a blend, but I would have reversed the percentage; I eventually called it 2008 Laurent-Perrier almost by default; our pH meter geek measured it at 2.86.

IMG_62001920×2680 373 KB

2018 THOMAS MOREY MORGEOT CHASSAGNE-MONTRACHET 1er Cru- blind; light yellow color; this had a lovely nose of fresh tropical fruit with kiwi most prevalent plus some nice honey dew melon and a touch of hazelnut; yellow apple and underlying oak influence was more noticeable on the palate; it was elegant and sophisticated and the honey taste was complimented by a like and kind texture; I guessed it to be a white Burg from around 2016, but that was as close as I could get; pH= 3.05

This wine was made by the brother of Vincent Morey who according to one source is looking to make a racy, fine-boned style of wine rather than the rounder, ampler style of his father and brother. His wines go through a long maturation stage and employ a maximum of 15% new oak.

IMG_62021920×2804 394 KB

IMG_62011054×2073 359 KB

1994 CHATEAU LEOVILLE BARTON ST. JULIEN- blind; my bring, decanted 2 hours; this had a seemingly youthful dark red purple color with an emphasis on the purple and massive aromas of earth and eucalyptus accented black currant and I’m thinking this is my bring with its distinct Cab profile; at first, I got a hint of brett, but it dissipated and gave way to more graphite and cedar and some blackberry fruit; it was soft and easy on the palate with its now integrated super soft tannins and along with its full body and layered depth, reminded me of the iron fist in a velvet glove analogy; for me, this release has been one of the stars of a not so great vintage and my last bottle of a treasured case. One of the others had it from the 1990s but nothing close to what it was. pH= 3.49

IMG_62031920×2560 505 KB

2015 TENUTA SAN GUIDO SASSICAIA BOLGHERI- blind; a blend of Cabernet Sauvignon and
Cabernet Franc that was aged in French oak barriques after completing ML in steel vats in November, and a small part in American oak, where it was aged for a period between the 20 and 25 months; this also had a youthful dark red purple color but with an emphasis on the red; the nose and taste offered some notes of leather, bell pepper and dark chocolate and a fruit profile that reminded me of unsweetened Welch’s grape juice, what I call “grapey”; it finished dry and I’m all over the place trying to ID the varietal{s}, old vs new world and on and on; I’ve been tricked many times by Nebbiolos from Piedmont emulating other varietals and I went with that for this wine and called it 2016 vintage. A good wine blind got a lot better once it was revealed= the subjectiveness prevailed. pH= 3.25

IMG_62041920×2560 390 KB

A fine evening indeed and I remain grateful and blessed to have these times with such great people.

Cheers,
Blake

IMG_61951920×1038 265 KB

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Great notes! That 1994 Leoville Barton is undoubtedly one of the stars of the vintage.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: As it was from the beginning. I remember tasting through many 1994s early on and it continually shined above most others.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Nice set and notes.

I’m not sure the story is quite right about the Moreys. Thomas was the primary winemaker for many years when the domaine was still held under his father, Bernard. He may have leaned a little leaner since, but if you know the old Bernard wines, they were made as vins de garde and have aged beautifully and vibrantly. Vincent, by contrast, basically had to learn how to make wine when the domaine split. And his wines tended to be fatter and less ageworthy. He also had a bad premox rate. In any case I buy a bit of Thomas when I can find it. Very good in both red and white.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: Thanks for your add on Jayson. Appreciated and appropriate.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: When did you last calibrate the pH meter? Those values seem systematically low…

posts.name: Nick Mitchell
posts.cooked: I was also thinking they were low then I saw the Sassicia at 3.25 and could not wrap my head around that.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: As to the pH meter readings, I am discussing this with our group tonight at our Monday night dinner and we’ll see what we need to do to get more accurate readings. Thanks for questioning it; I do agree they seem systematically low.

posts.name: Nick Mitchell
posts.cooked: Hanna Inst. sells cleaning and calibration packets that are easy to use and will get you dialed in if needed.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: We properly calibrated the pH meter prior to testing the wines and did come up with some values that seemed more accurate {higher in this case}. Notes forthcoming in another thread in a day or 2.
